_comment: Geologic time units, oldest first. Times are in Ma (million years ago). Units form a hierarchy by 'rank' (eon > era > period) linked via 'parentId'. The timeline renders one stacked row per rank. Colors of Phanerozoic units follow the official ICS International Chronostratigraphic Chart; Precambrian sub-unit colors are approximate ICS-family tints. 'overview' is a longer free-text introduction shown as a main paragraph above the detailed facts; omit it to hide that section. 'info' fields are free-form — add or remove keys as you like, the panel renders whatever is present. 'images' paths are relative to the site root; missing images render as labeled placeholders. Optional 'background' (image path) and 'audio' (looping track path, e.g. assets/audio/<id>.mp3) drive the immersive page background and ambient sound when a unit is focused; both are optional and inherit from the parent unit when omitted (background also falls back to images[0], then to a color gradient; audio falls back to silence). To add a unit: copy any entry, set its 'rank' and 'parentId', and fill it in.

units.name: Hadean

info.atmosphere: No free oxygen. A hot haze of carbon dioxide, water vapor, nitrogen and volcanic gases. Early on, possibly steam from a magma ocean.
info.terrain: Molten or freshly solidified crust, constant heavy bombardment by asteroids. The Moon forms from a giant impact early in this eon.
info.notable: Oceans likely condense near the end of the eon.

units.name: Archean
units.overview: Archean overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Still essentially oxygen-free: methane, CO2, nitrogen. The sky may have been orange with organic haze.
info.terrain: First stable continental cores (cratons) form. Global oceans, scattered volcanic islands.
info.flora: No plants, but life begins: single-celled prokaryotes and, later, photosynthesizing cyanobacteria building stromatolite mounds.
info.notable: Oldest widely accepted fossils (~3.5 billion years) date from this eon.

units.name: Eoarchean
units.overview: Eoarchean overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: Earliest era of the Archean; some of the oldest known rocks and possible traces of life.

units.name: Paleoarchean
units.overview: Paleoarchean overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: Oldest well-preserved microfossils and stromatolites date from this era.

units.name: Mesoarchean
units.overview: Mesoarchean overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: Continental crust grows; widespread stromatolite reefs.

units.name: Neoarchean
units.overview: Neoarchean overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: Oxygenic photosynthesis well underway, setting the stage for the Great Oxidation Event.

units.name: Proterozoic
units.overview: Proterozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: The Great Oxidation Event (~2.4 billion years ago) introduces free oxygen, rusting the oceans' iron into banded iron formations.
info.terrain: Continents grow and assemble into supercontinents (Columbia, Rodinia). At least two 'Snowball Earth' global glaciations.
info.flora: Single cells dominate for most of the eon; complex eukaryotic cells and, at the very end, the first soft-bodied multicellular life (Ediacaran biota).
info.notable: Roughly half of Earth's entire history fits inside this one eon.

units.name: Paleoproterozoic
units.overview: Paleoproterozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: The Great Oxidation Event and the first supercontinent (Columbia) fall in this era.

units.name: Mesoproterozoic
units.overview: Mesoproterozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: Supercontinent Rodinia assembles; eukaryotic life diversifies.

units.name: Neoproterozoic
units.overview: Neoproterozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: Snowball Earth glaciations and the first complex multicellular life (Ediacaran biota).

units.name: Siderian
units.overview: Siderian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Banded iron formations peak as oxygen rusts the oceans.

units.name: Rhyacian
units.overview: Rhyacian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: The Huronian glaciation, one of the longest ice ages in Earth's history.

units.name: Orosirian
units.overview: Orosirian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Intense mountain building and large asteroid impacts.

units.name: Statherian
units.overview: Statherian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Supercontinent Columbia stabilizes; first eukaryotic cells appear.

units.name: Calymmian
units.overview: Calymmian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Expansion of platform covers as Columbia breaks up.

units.name: Ectasian
units.overview: Ectasian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Earliest known sexually reproducing organisms.

units.name: Stenian
units.overview: Stenian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Supercontinent Rodinia forms along narrow metamorphic belts.

units.name: Tonian
units.overview: Tonian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: Rodinia begins to rift apart; first possible animals (sponges).

units.name: Cryogenian
units.overview: Cryogenian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: The most severe 'Snowball Earth' glaciations, ice possibly reaching the equator.

units.name: Ediacaran
units.overview: Ediacaran overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit.

info.notable: The Ediacaran biota: Earth's first large, soft-bodied multicellular life.

units.name: Phanerozoic
units.overview: Phanerozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat.

info.atmosphere: Oxygen-rich, supporting large animals and, eventually, fire.
info.terrain: The eon of 'visible life' — continents drift into modern positions; life colonizes and transforms the land.
info.flora: From the first land plants to flowering forests and grasslands.
info.notable: Spans the Paleozoic, Mesozoic and Cenozoic — the last ~12% of Earth's history, holding nearly all familiar fossils.

units.name: Paleozoic
units.overview: Paleozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: 'Old life': from the Cambrian Explosion to the colonization of land, ending with the Permian 'Great Dying'.

units.name: Mesozoic
units.overview: Mesozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: The 'age of reptiles' and dinosaurs, ending with the Chicxulub asteroid impact 66 Ma.

units.name: Cenozoic
units.overview: Cenozoic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua.

info.notable: The 'age of mammals' (and, late in the era, humans).

units.name: Cambrian
units.overview: Cambrian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Oxygen rising but still below modern levels; high CO2 and a greenhouse climate.
info.terrain: Life is confined to the seas; the land is bare rock and microbial crusts. Continents scattered, Gondwana assembling.
info.flora: Marine algae only — no land plants yet.
info.notable: The Cambrian Explosion: almost every major animal body plan appears in the fossil record, including the first eyes and shells.

units.name: Ordovician
units.overview: Ordovician overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Very high CO2, yet ending in a sharp ice age and mass extinction.
info.terrain: Vast shallow seas flood the continents — prime real estate for reefs and trilobites.
info.flora: First tiny non-vascular plants (liverwort-like) creep onto land.
info.notable: Ends with the first of the 'Big Five' mass extinctions.

units.name: Silurian
units.overview: Silurian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Climate stabilizes and warms after the Ordovician ice age.
info.terrain: Coral reefs expand; land is being colonized along rivers and coasts.
info.flora: First vascular plants (like Cooksonia) stand a few centimeters tall.
info.notable: First air-breathing animals on land: millipede-like arthropods.

units.name: Devonian
units.overview: Devonian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Warm greenhouse world; oxygen climbing as forests appear.
info.terrain: The 'Age of Fishes' in the seas; on land, the first true forests and soils transform the landscape.
info.flora: First trees (Archaeopteris) and first seed plants.
info.notable: Fish grow limbs: tetrapods like Tiktaalik haul out of the water.

units.name: Carboniferous
units.overview: Carboniferous overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Oxygen peaks around 30–35% — the highest in Earth's history — fueling giant insects.
info.terrain: Endless tropical swamp forests whose buried remains become most of the world's coal.
info.flora: Towering lycopod 'scale trees', horsetails and tree ferns.
info.notable: Dragonflies with 70 cm wingspans; the first reptiles and the amniote egg.

units.name: Permian
units.overview: Permian overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Drying and warming as the supercontinent Pangaea locks together; vast deserts in its interior.
info.terrain: One supercontinent, one super-ocean (Panthalassa). Huge inland dunes and salt flats.
info.flora: Seed ferns and early conifers replace the coal swamps.
info.notable: Ends with the Great Dying — the worst mass extinction ever, erasing ~90% of marine species.

units.name: Triassic
units.overview: Triassic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Hot and dry, with monsoon extremes. CO2 high, oxygen relatively low after the Great Dying.
info.terrain: All land fused into Pangaea, centered on the equator. Vast red-rock deserts inland, conifer woodlands near coasts.
info.flora: Conifers, cycads, ginkgoes and seed ferns. No grass, no flowers — the ground cover is ferns and horsetails.
info.notable: Life rebuilds after the Great Dying. The first dinosaurs, first pterosaurs, first mammals and first turtles all appear here.

units.name: Jurassic
units.overview: Jurassic overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Warm, humid greenhouse with no polar ice caps. CO2 several times modern levels.
info.terrain: Pangaea splits apart; the young Atlantic Ocean opens. Shallow seas flood continental edges; lush vegetated lowlands.
info.flora: Conifer forests with an understory of ferns, cycads and ginkgoes. Still no flowering plants.
info.notable: The golden age of giant sauropods. First birds (Archaeopteryx) take wing late in the period.

units.name: Cretaceous
units.overview: Cretaceous overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Very warm for most of the period — crocodiles near the poles, sea levels up to 200 m higher than today.
info.terrain: Continents drift toward familiar positions. Huge shallow seas split North America and Europe; chalk forms from plankton (hence the name).
info.flora: The revolution: flowering plants appear and spread explosively, co-evolving with pollinating insects. Magnolias, figs and the first broadleaf forests.
info.notable: Ends abruptly 66 million years ago when the Chicxulub asteroid strikes, ending the non-avian dinosaurs.

units.name: Paleogene
units.overview: Paleogene overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Starts hot (a major temperature spike ~56 Ma), then a long cooling trend; Antarctica freezes over near the end.
info.terrain: India crashes into Asia and the Himalayas begin to rise. Dense forests reach high latitudes early on.
info.flora: Flowering plants and tropical forests dominate; grasses exist but grasslands are not yet widespread.
info.notable: Mammals inherit the Earth: whales return to the sea, bats take to the air, primates take to the trees.

units.name: Neogene
units.overview: Neogene overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Cooling and drying continues; CO2 drops toward modern values.
info.terrain: Continents essentially in modern positions. Grasslands and savannas spread across the world for the first time.
info.flora: The age of grass. Open savannas reshape evolution — grazers get tall teeth, predators get fast.
info.notable: Apes diversify; hominins split from chimpanzees and begin walking upright in Africa.

units.name: Quaternary
units.overview: Quaternary overview — Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit, sed do eiusmod tempor incididunt ut labore et dolore magna aliqua. Ut enim ad minim veniam, quis nostrud exercitation ullamco laboris nisi ut aliquip ex ea commodo consequat. Duis aute irure dolor in reprehenderit in voluptate velit esse cillum dolore eu fugiat nulla pariatur.

info.atmosphere: Ice-age world: CO2 and temperature swing with glacial cycles every ~100,000 years.
info.terrain: Ice sheets repeatedly bury the northern continents, sea levels rise and fall by over 100 m, carving today's landscapes.
info.flora: Modern flora; tundra and steppe expand during glacials, forests during interglacials.
info.notable: Homo sapiens appears (~300,000 years ago), spreads worldwide, and starts building timelines about it.
